analogy: Think of "Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance" through the chapter's own opening images: 2.1 I 2.1 I 2.1 I 2.1 I 2.1 INTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION In Chapters 5 and 7 (Class XI), the notion of potential energy was introduced. When an external force does work in taking a body from a point to another against a... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 2.1 I 2.1 I 2.1 I 2.1 I 2.1 INTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION NTRODUCTION In Chapters 5 and 7 (Class XI), the notion of potential energy was introduced.

The text explains that when an external force does work in taking a body from a point to another against a force like spring force or gravitational force, that work gets stored as potential energy of the body.

The text explains that when the external force is removed, the body moves, gaining kinetic energy and losing an equal amount of potential energy.

The text explains that the sum of kinetic and potential energies is thus conserved.

The text explains that forces of this kind are called conservative forces.

The text explains that spring force and gravitational force are examples of conservative forces.

The text explains that coulomb force between two (stationary) charges is also a conservative force.

The text explains that this is not surprising, since both have inverse-square dependence on distance and differ mainly in the proportionality constants – the masses in the gravitational law are replaced by charges in Coulomb’s law.

The text explains that thus, like the potential energy of a mass in a gravitational field, we can define electrostatic potential energy of a charge in an electrostatic field.

The text explains that consider an electrostatic field E E E E E due to some charge configuration.

The text explains that first, for simplicity, consider the field E E E E E due to a charge Q placed at the origin.

The text explains that now, imagine that we bring a test charge q from a point R to a point P against the repulsive force on it due to the charge Q.

The text explains that with reference Chapter Two ELECTROSTATIC POTENTIAL AND CAPACITANCE

The text explains that 2.1, this will happen if Q and q are both positive or both negative.

The text explains that first, we assume that the test charge q is so small that it does not disturb the original configuration, namely the charge Q at the origin (or else, we keep Q fixed at the origin by some unspecified force).

The text explains that second, in bringing the charge q from R to P, we apply an external force Fext just enough to counter the repulsive electric force FE (i.e, Fext= –FE).

The text explains that since work done is independent of the path, we choose a convenient path – along the radial direction from infinity to the point P.

The text explains that this is equivalent to saying that work done by the electrostatic force in bringing the unit positive charge form infinity to the point P is positive. [This is as it should be, since for Q < 0, the force on a unit positive test charge is attractive, so that the electrostatic force and the displacement (from infinity to P) are in the same direction.] Finally, we note that Eq.

The text explains that it is characterised by a dipole moment vector p whose magnitude is q × 2a and which points in the direction from –q to q (Fig.

The text explains that (2.18) If we have a continuous charge distribution characterised by a charge density r (r), we divide it, as before, into small volume elements each of size Dv and carrying a charge rDv.

The text explains that to move a unit test charge against the direction of the component of the field, work would have to be done.

The text explains that 2.12) with potential values V and V + d V, where d V is the change in V in the direction of the electric field E.

The text explains that thus, |E| d l = V – (V + dV )= – dV i.e., |E|= − δ δ V l (2.20) Since dV is negative, dV = – | dV|. we can rewrite Eq (2.20) as E = − = + δ δ δ δ V l V l (2.21) We thus arrive at two important conclusions concerning the relation between electric field and potential: (i) Electric field is in the direction in which the potential decreases steepest.

The text explains that the potential energy FIGURE 2.13 Potential energy of a system of charges q1 and q2 is directly proportional to the product of charges and inversely to the distance between them.

The text explains that 2.8 POTENTIAL ENERGY IN AN EXTERNAL FIELD 2.8.1 Potential energy of a single charge In Section 2.7, the source of the electric field was specified – the charges and their locations - and the potential energy of the system of those charges was determined.

The text explains that e is produced by sources external to the given charge(s).The external sources may be known, but often they are unknown or unspecified; what is specified is the electric field E or the electrostatic potential V due to the external sources.

The text explains that we assume that the charge q does not significantly affect the sources producing the external field.

The text explains that this is true if q is very small, or the external sources are held fixed by other unspecified forces.

The text explains that even if q is finite, its influence on the external sources may still be ignored in the situation when very strong sources far away at infinity produce a finite field E in the region of interest.

The text explains that note again that we are interested in determining the potential energy of a given charge q (and later, a system of charges) in the external field; we are not interested in the potential energy of the sources producing the external electric field.

The text explains that a mole of this substance is polarised (at low temperature) by applying a strong electrostatic field of magnitude 106 V m–1.

The text explains that the direction of the field is suddenly changed by an angle of 60º.

The text explains that estimate the heat released by the substance in aligning its dipoles along the new direction of the field.

The text explains that the free electrons form a kind of ‘gas’; they collide with each other and with the ions, and move randomly in different directions.

The text explains that in an external electric field, they drift against the direction of the field.

The text explains that (For a conductor without any surface charge density, field is zero even at the surface.) See result 5.

The text explains that the interior of a conductor can have no excess charge in the static situation A neutral conductor has equal amounts of positive and negative charges in every small volume or surface element.

The text explains that electric field at the surface of a charged conductor 0 ˆ σ ε = E n (2.35) where s is the surface charge density and ˆn is a unit vector normal to the surface in the outward direction.

The text explains that physics 66 In an external electric field, the positive and negative charges of a non- polar molecule are displaced in opposite directions.

The text explains that we consider only the simple situation when the induced dipole moment is in the direction of the field and is proportional to the field strength.

The text explains that when summed overall the molecules, there is then a net dipole moment in the direction of the external field, i.e., the dielectric is polarised.

The text explains that electrostatic Potential and Capacitance 67 every volume element Dv of the slab has a dipole moment P Dv in the direction of the field.

The text explains that however, at the surfaces of the dielectric normal to the electric field, there is evidently a net charge density.

The text explains that we should note that the surface charge density ± sp arises from bound (not free charges) in the dielectric.

The text explains that (This follows from the direct proportionality between field and charge implied by Coulomb’s law and the superposition principle.) Now, potential difference V is the work done per unit positive charge in taking a small test charge from the conductor 2 to 1 against the field.

The text explains that the capacitance C depends only on the FIGURE 2.23 A uniformly polarised dielectric amounts to induced surface charge density, but no volume charge density.

The text explains that since d is much smaller than the linear dimension of the plates (d2 << A), we can use the result on electric field by an infinite plane sheet of uniform surface charge density (Section 1.15).

The text explains that plate 1 has surface charge density s = Q/A and plate 2 has a surface charge density – s.

The text explains that electrostatic Potential and Capacitance 69 Outer region II (region below the plate 2), 0 0 0 2 2 E σ σ ε ε = − = (2.40) In the inner region between the plates 1 and 2, the electric fields due to the two charged plates add up, giving 0 0 0 0 2 2 Q E A σ σ σ ε ε ε ε = + = = (2.41) The direction of electric field is from the positive to the negative plate.

The text explains that the charge on the plates is ±Q, corresponding to the charge density ± s (with s = Q/A).

The text explains that the electric field in the dielectric then corresponds to the case when the net surface charge density on the plates is ±( s – sp ).
